title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
Mutual Fund Fees and Charges
When you invest in a mutual fund, you’re essentially handing your money to experts who manage it on your behalf. Naturally, this service comes with a cost. These mutual fund fees cover the fund manager’s expertise, along with other expenses needed to run the scheme. While the charges may seem small, they can influence how much you actually earn over time. Let us understand mutual fund charges in detail and how they affect long-term returns.
What Are Mutual Fund Fees and Charges?
Whenever you invest in a mutual fund, an Asset Management Company (AMC) and its fund manager manage your money. They’re backed by research analysts and market specialists who track markets, manage risks, and make investment decisions every day. Running this entire setup comes with a cost, which is why AMCs levy SEBI-regulated mutual fund fees. These expenses fall into two broad categories1:
One-time charges apply at the time of starting your investment, similar to an entry fee.
Recurring charges are regular costs, deducted daily, quarterly, or yearly, to cover ongoing portfolio management, advisory work, marketing, and other operational expenses.
Some other mutual fund charges include management fees, service and distribution charges, account costs, and switching fees.
Expense Ratio in Mutual Funds
The expense ratio is basically the ongoing fee you pay for the management of a mutual fund. It’s shown as a percentage of the fund’s daily assets and is charged annually by the AMC so that the fund can run efficiently2.
What Is Included in the Expense Ratio2?
The expense ratio covers all essential running costs of the fund. This includes the fund manager’s compensation, administrative expenses, and promotional or distribution-related costs etc. The percentage is calculated by dividing the total expenses of the scheme by the AMC’s total AUM (Assets Under Management).
Fees Not Included in Expense Ratio3
Additional mutual fund costs not covered in the expense ratio include trading costs paid to brokers whenever the fund buys or sells securities, tax outflows from gains booked inside the portfolio, and the impact of holding surplus cash to manage redemptions. Funds investing overseas may also incur additional international transaction and compliance expenses.
How Is Expense Ratio Calculated?6
To calculate the expense ratio, fund houses use the Total Expense Ratio (TER) formula. It’s simply the total annual expenses of a scheme divided by its average net assets, multiplied by 100.
For example, imagine a mutual fund with assets worth ₹800 crore and total yearly costs of ₹16 crore. Using the formula: ₹16 crore ÷ ₹800 crore = 2%
This means investors in that scheme effectively pay a 2% annual fee, which the fund reflects through daily NAV adjustments ..
So, if you put ₹6,000 into a fund with a 2% expense ratio, the approximate daily deduction would be: 2% ÷ 365 ≈ 0.00548% per day.
You’re charged only for the period you remain invested, but this small daily cut does chip away at your returns over time. That’s why funds with a lower expense ratio often work better, as they leave more of your money to compound.
Direct vs Regular Plans: Difference in Mutual Fund Charges
Every mutual fund is offered in two versions—direct and regular1. With a direct plan, you invest straight through the AMC, which keeps costs low because no commission is paid to a distributor1. However, selecting the right fund demands time, research, and comfort with market trends, and that’s where intermediaries step in1.
If you prefer expert guidance, you can choose a regular plan. The advice comes with a commission, which is added to the scheme’s expense ratio, making it slightly costlier4. The Net Asset Value (NAV) of the fund is subsequently declared each day, after adjusting for these expenses. Hence, the NAV of a direct plan generally ends up higher than that of a regular plan4.
Impact of Distributor Commission on Returns
When you choose a regular mutual fund plan, a part of your investment goes toward paying the distributor’s commission4. This cost is added to the expense ratio, so your returns reduce slightly over time4. It may not seem like much in a single year, but over the long term, even a small recurring commission can significantly lower your overall wealth compared to a direct plan where no such fee is charged4.
Maximum Expense Ratio Limits Prescribed by SEBI5
SEBI has set clear caps on how much fund houses can charge as their Total Expense Ratio (TER). The larger the fund’s AUM, the lower the TER it is allowed to levy.
Note: Fund houses can charge an extra 0.30 basis points if the new inflows from retail investors from beyond top 30 cities (B30) are at least -
(a) 30% of gross new inflows in the scheme or
(b) 15% of the scheme’s average assets under management (year to date), whichever is the highest5. .
How Mutual Fund Fees Affect Long-Term Returns?
Mutual fund charges, especially the expense ratio, play a quiet but powerful role in shaping your long-term wealth. Even a small fee can shave off part of your yearly gains. For instance, if a scheme earns 9% before expenses and the expense ratio is 1%, your actual return drops to 8%.
Over many years, this difference slows down compounding because a smaller portion of your money gets reinvested. That’s why long-term investors often prefer funds with lower ongoing costs, as they help more of your returns stay with you.
It’s also smart to review your funds periodically to ensure the performance and the expense ratio still justify staying invested.
Key Takeaway
Mutual fund fees cover the cost of managing your money, and they directly influence how much you earn over time.
Investors face one-time charges like transaction fees and recurring charges such as the annual expense ratio.
Common fees include exit load for early redemption
The expense ratio is a key cost that includes fund management, operations, and distribution, but excludes trading costs and certain other expenses.
Direct plans have lower charges than regular plans, which helps them maintain a higher net asset value over time.
SEBI has set a limit on how much fund houses can charge based on their Total Expense Ratio.
Higher mutual fund charges eat into long-term returns, making low-cost funds more beneficial for long-term compounding.
Conclusion
Understanding mutual fund fees helps you see what you’re actually paying for and how those costs shape your long-term returns. The Consolidated Account Statement of the mutual fund contains complete details. When you know how each fee works, you can pick smarter options and get more value from your investment plans.
FAQs
What are the fees charged in mutual funds?
Mutual funds primarily involve one-time charges, like the exit load which may apply if you redeem early.
Are mutual fund fees charged monthly or annually?
Mutual fund fee periodicity varies by charge type , for instance  the expense ratio is an annual charge, but it’s deducted daily from the fund’s NAV7. Other fees, such as exit load, applies only when you redeem or make a qualifying investment1.
Which mutual fund fees are not part of the expense ratio?
Trading costs, taxes on gains from portfolio transactions, impact on high cash reserves, and expenses related to foreign investments are not included in the expense ratio3.
Disclaimer
The content provided is for general informational purposes only. The material is compiled from publicly available sources, internal insights, and other information deemed reliable. While reasonable care has been taken in compiling the information, Bajaj Life Insurance Limited (Formerly known as Bajaj Allianz Life Insurance Company Limited) assumes no liability for its accuracy. The opinions expressed do not constitute formal recommendations, professional advice, or definitive guidance. Readers are encouraged to conduct their own due diligence and are advised to seek independent professional or expert advice before making any financial or investment decisions based on the content. Any illustrations included are for conceptual clarity only and do not reflect the actual performance of any product or offering.

They’re backed by research analysts and market specialists who track markets, manage risks, and make investment decisions every day. Running this entire setup comes with a cost, which is why AMCs levy SEBI-regulated mutual fund fees. These expenses fall into two broad categories1:
1. One-time charges apply at the time of starting your investment, similar to an entry fee. 2. Recurring charges are regular costs, deducted daily, quarterly, or yearly, to cover ongoing
portfolio management, advisory work, marketing, and other operational expenses.
Some other mutual fund charges include management fees, service and distribution charges, account costs, and switching fees.

1. What Is Included in the Expense Ratio2?
The expense ratio covers all essential running costs of the fund. This includes the fund manager’s compensation, administrative expenses, and promotional or distribution-related costs etc. The percentage is calculated by dividing the total expenses of the scheme by the AMC’s total AUM (Assets Under Management). 2. Fees Not Included in Expense Ratio3
Additional mutual fund costs not covered in the expense ratio include trading costs paid to brokers whenever the fund buys or sells securities, tax outflows from gains booked inside the portfolio, and the impact of holding surplus cash to manage redemptions. Funds investing overseas may also incur additional international transaction and compliance expenses.

This means investors in that scheme effectively pay a 2% annual fee, which the fund reflects through daily NAV adjustments ..
So, if you put ₹6,000 into a fund with a 2% expense ratio, the approximate daily deduction would be: 2% ÷ 365 ≈ 0.00548% per day.
You’re charged only for the period you remain invested, but this small daily cut does chip away at your returns over time. That’s why funds with a lower expense ratio often work better, as they leave more of your money to compound.
1. Direct vs Regular Plans: Difference in Mutual Fund Charges
Every mutual fund is offered in two versions—direct and regular1. With a direct plan, you invest straight through the AMC, which keeps costs low because no commission is paid to a distributor1. However, selecting the right fund demands time, research, and comfort with market trends, and that’s where intermediaries step in1.

The Net Asset Value (NAV) of the fund is subsequently declared each day, after adjusting for these expenses. Hence, the NAV of a direct plan generally ends up higher than that of a regular plan4.
Impact of Distributor Commission on Returns
When you choose a regular mutual fund plan, a part of your investment goes toward paying the distributor’s commission4. Maximum Expense Ratio Limits Prescribed by SEBI5
SEBI has set clear caps on how much fund houses can charge as their Total Expense Ratio (TER). The larger the fund’s AUM, the lower the TER it is allowed to levy.
Asset Size (AUM) Equity Funds (Max TER) (percentage of net daily assets)
Debt Funds (Max TER) (percentage of net daily assets) Up to ₹500 crore 2.25% 2.00% ₹500 - 750 crore 2.00% 1.75% ₹750 - 2,000 crore 1.75% 1.50% ₹2,000 – 5,000 crore 1.60% 1.35% ₹5,000 – 10,000 crore 1.50% 1.25%
₹10,000 – 50,000 crore Reduces by 0.05% for every ₹5,000 crore increase of daily net assets or part thereof.
Reduces by 0.05% for every ₹5,000 crore increase of daily net assets or part therefof. Above ₹50,000 crore 1.05% 0.80%
Note: Fund houses can charge an extra 0.30 basis points if the new inflows from retail investors from beyond top 30 cities (B30) are at least -
(a) 30% of gross new inflows in the scheme or
(b) 15% of the scheme’s average assets under management (year to date), whichever is the highest5. .
How Mutual Fund Fees Affect Long-Term Returns?
Mutual fund charges, especially the expense ratio, play a quiet but powerful role in shaping your long-term wealth. Even a small fee can shave off part of your yearly gains. For instance, if a scheme earns 9% before expenses and the expense ratio is 1%, your actual return drops to 8%.
Over many years, this difference slows down compounding because a smaller portion of your money gets reinvested. That’s why long-term investors often prefer funds with lower ongoing costs, as they help more of your returns stay with you.
It’s also smart to review your funds periodically to ensure the performance and the expense ratio still justify staying invested.
Key Takeaway
1. Mutual fund fees cover the cost of managing your money, and they directly influence how
much you earn over time. 2. Investors face one-time charges like transaction fees and recurring charges such as the
annual expense ratio. 3. Common fees include exit load for early redemption 4. The expense ratio is a key cost that includes fund management, operations, and distribution,
but excludes trading costs and certain other expenses. 5. Direct plans have lower charges than regular plans, which helps them maintain a higher net
asset value over time. 6. SEBI has set a limit on how much fund houses can charge based on their Total Expense Ratio. 7. Higher mutual fund charges eat into long-term returns, making low-cost funds more
beneficial for long-term compounding.
Conclusion
Understanding mutual fund fees helps you see what you’re actually paying for and how those costs shape your long-term returns. The Consolidated Account Statement of the mutual fund contains complete details. When you know how each fee works, you can pick smarter options and get more value from your investment plans.

1. What are the fees charged in mutual funds?
Mutual funds primarily involve one-time charges, like the exit load which may apply if you redeem early. 2. Are mutual fund fees charged monthly or annually?
Mutual fund fee periodicity varies by charge type , for instance the expense ratio is an annual charge, but it’s deducted daily from the fund’s NAV7. Other fees, such as exit load, applies only when you redeem or make a qualifying investment1. 3. Which mutual fund fees are not part of the expense ratio?
